replies.author: @Commander.Starfleet
replies.text:
​@ddding9518

Jurchen were a number of East Asian Tungusic-speaking people. They lived in northeastern China, also known as Manchuria, before the 18th century. The Jurchens were renamed Manchus in 1635 by Hong Taiji.

The Jurchens are chiefly known for producing the Jin (1115–1234) and *Qing* (1636–1912) conquest dynasties on the Chinese territory.

replies.author: @一个说话大声的中国人
replies.text:
Regarding Chinese civilization, whether it has died and been reassembled again and again, many people not only hold this word of "died" as a tenet but even regard it as an article of faith.
Never mind bother show people a thing or two, which you've or your parents or teachers have seen, that has died and been reassembled again and again.
Aha, you must be talking about Jews' God and his adultery Asian boy Jesus. Jesus died of crucifixion and been resurrected by his daddy.
Look, if Chinese civilization has died, it needed to be resurrected, not reassembled.
English, do you know English?
Logic, do you know Logic?
Jesus, do you know Jesus?
Do you even know who reassembled Chinese civilization again and again?
After all, "been reassembled" is passive form in English. Do you know that by whom Chinese civilization has been reassembled again and again?
Is your answer Jesus' daddy, Jews' God?
The righteous answer must be Chinese, Chinese have reassembled Chinese civilization again and again.
Chinese civilization has fallen apart or been broken several times, the most obvious, the governance were broken, or by two of the 56 minorities, the Mongols and the Manchus.
So, the statement of that Chinese civilization has died and been reassembled again and again is both illogical and bad English, because dead needs resurrection like Jesus got, not reassemble, and reassembled needs a subject does the reassembling. 
So, a correct English is that Chinese civilization was broken again and again, Chinese have reassembled Chinese civilization again and again, because two neighbors the Mongols and the Manchus broke Chinese civilization in some ways. 
In the case of the Mongols and the Manchus, they revered Confucius even more than the Chinese did.
So, obviously, not only Confucius thought and Chinese language didn't died, Confucius and Chinese need no reassemble. 
How could Chinese civilization die while Confucius thought stays alive?
Consider Greece: the Greeks are Christians, and the Greek gods died out long ago.
Consider Europe: the Europeans' God is Christ, and the old European gods died out long ago; Europeans worship Christ.
FYI, Confucius thought stays alive even today.
Oh, you might not realize that Christianity is an Asian religion, because Christ was Asian—or more precisely, Middle Eastern, and the Middle East is in Asia. The Virgin Mary was Asian, as was her husband, Joseph the carpenter; both the Jewish people and the Jewish God are Asian.
What died or broken in Chinese civilization was the government or Board of Directors and needed reassemble.
Yet, the Mongols and the Manchus, they did not impose their own religions or languages ​​upon the Chinese people.
Brow an American term, Chinese have kept their way of life except clothing and hair styles.
In fact, the various honorific titles bestowed upon Confucius originated with the Mongols and the Manchus. There appear to be two reasons for this: first, they genuinely revered Confucius; and second, they needed him to facilitate their rule over 
China. During the periods of Mongol and Manchu rule, Chinese, Mongols, and Manchus alike spoke Chinese; even at the imperial court, Chinese was the language used by Mongol and Manchu rulers. 
Like the languages ​​and deities of China's other fifty-six ethnic minorities, the Mongol and Manchu languages ​​and gods were specific to their own cultures; the Chinese people were under no obligation to learn Mongol or Manchu, nor were they required to worship Mongol or Manchu deities. 
Conversely, both Mongols and Manchus were compelled to learn Chinese and worship or respect, sincere or not, Confucius in order to govern the Chinese effectively.

comments.author: @shoppingaccount415
comments.text: China was first unified in the Shang dynasty (1600–1046 BCE) China's second dynasty, the Zhou dynasty (1050-256BCE) invented the concept of the Mandate of Heaven, (Tian Ming) which was not hereditary, and had nothing to do with any gods. China has never had a state religion, which is a big reason why China is still around. And the most recent loss of Chinese national sovereignty, was the Century of Humiliation. which I have never heard mentioned by someone with a British accent.

comments.text: 西方人在塑造世界观的时候惯用思维是用民族作为国家和文化区分，用民族和血统作为人群区分，如果一个民族没有成立一个国家就认为是亡国，文化断代，就认为这个民族的文明消散了。然而中国在春秋时期的时候，就有不同的观点：“诸侯用夷礼，则夷之；进于中国，则中国之”。就是说你是文明还是野蛮，不看你什么民族，而是看你使用什么礼仪和行为。这和中华文化类似，不以极端民族性排外，开放包容，以个人品德和行为作为是否野蛮和文明的标准，融入了中华这整个文化圈，你就不是外人。这也是为什么中华文化能在各种烈度下的战争危机中幸存下来，并持续进化发展。

comments.author: @Dermisc
comments.text:
Actually, the Yellow River is no longer the colour of milky tea. Civil engineering is finally managing to tame that river, after 3000 years of trying.

China was never about any single regime, empire, or system of governance. It is, at its core, a collectively agreeable philosophy that promotes the greater good of its inhabitants. It is no accident that Chinese people chose the communists after a century of bloodshed. The country and its people have managed, after some decades of chiseling and polishing, to shape a loosely fitting political ideology into a regime that is at peace with the nation's core philosophies and values.

comments.author: @LittleAmyHe
comments.text:
I just wanted to add the context that Han culture was nearly destroyed by the rule of Manchurians. They did adopt some but they spent most of their energy separating Han people from their own clothing and even Manchurian clothing. For a long time Han history and culture was considered shameful and “poor.” Han people had a lot of trouble reconnecting with their own culture until very, very recently. I remember even in 2019 people were being made fun of for wearing Han clothing. Ethnic minorities were largely unaffected by Manchurian rule partly due to them being isolated and also there not being many of them to begin with at the start of their rule. 

It’s so interesting to see that the Han Kingdom actually adopted Chu Kingdom culture because of how much the Chu civilization were idolized as soon as they took Chu over. And dynasties ended with Manchurians, who were the only ones purposefully trying to erase Han culture.

comments.author: @魯絲
comments.text: China civilization plugged the chaos not just three times. After East Han dynasty in 220 AD, China fell into 360+ years splits, which called "Wei Jin Northern Southern Dynasties" periods, before China reunited by Sui dynasty in 581 again. In between, it went through Three kingdoms, West Jin, 5 Hu 16 Kingdoms (mainly consisted of 5 steppe ethnics) at North, and West Jin fled to south establish East Jin. Later the North united by Xian Bei ethnic, but split again. Standoff with North, the south went through 4 States after East Jin fell. China experienced the big unification of Sui, Tang and prosperity. After collapse of Tang dynasty in 907 AD, China went through another the chaos in 70 years, called "Five dynasty and 10 Kingdom" before unification of Song dynasty. Song dynasty also survived for 300 years, and it was coexisting with another three minority dynasties, Liao, Jin and XiXia. All in all, they all become the big family of HuaXia China 华夏。

comments.author: @yoingen
comments.text: Before the unification under Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the vassal states were not independent sovereign nations in the modern sense. Established under the enfeoffment system of the Zhou Dynasty, they shared the Rites of Zhou, a common writing system, and ancestral lineages, collectively identifying themselves as Huaxia—one people bound by kinship. The Great Wall built in that era was constructed to fend off nomadic groups such as the Xiongnu, who were not regarded as part of "our own people" at the time. In ancient China, the concepts of "Zhonghua (China)" and "Huaxia" were fluid and inclusive. Any regime that upheld China’s traditional governance norms and inherited the orthodox dynastic lineage and culture would be recognized as a legitimate Chinese dynasty.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The Huaxia framing — shared rites and lineage as identity rather than borders — is exactly the lens that makes the "three resets" question more complicated and more interesting. Thank you for that. 🌿

And even if our community is very small, we have a recorded history in writing, used handmade paper, drank tea, the first recorded king who came to the throne in 1st c appointed court chroniclers too and has continued since. Now, many institutionlised religions and mass cultures are taking over us, yet I can't help but have chills and goosebumps listening about a country we may or may not be related with.

comments.author: @hfdennycheng9010
comments.text:
何謂中國？

2個共和國
56個民族
8種語言
3種宗教
2種文字
1個國家

2個共和國=中華人民共和國、中華民國
56個民族=漢、滿、蒙、回、藏、朝鮮、維吾爾...
8種語言=國語、粵語、吳語、閩語、徽語、湘語、晉語、客家語
3種宗教=儒家、佛教、道教
2種文字=繁體漢字、簡体漢字
1個國家=中國

comments.author: @shiximo-77
comments.text:
0:38 Let's start from the very first point. You believe that the Chinese writing system is less than 3000 years old, right?
Oracle bone script, as a script with numerous unearthed artefacts, already possessed a complete linguistic capacity and 1000+ of characters at least 3300 years ago. Its initial invention certainly predates this period. Furthermore, the shapes of oracle bone characters evolved and expanded, gradually becoming the Chinese characters today. If apply the same argument, English only existed in the 16th century, meaning it's less than 500 years.

replies.author: @shiximo-77
@WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That distinction between oracle bone script's emergence and its evolution into the modern form deserved more space than the film gave it — 3300 years is the more defensible anchor. The real question worth opening up: does the script's continuity make the political resets feel smaller, or does it make them more striking by contrast?

replies.author: @shiximo-77
replies.text: The view that a change in political system inevitably leads to a different civilisation is a very Western perspective, and I don't even consider it a Western academic view. Many cultures exist within a single civilisation, coexisting in different times and places,and the main culture will learn from and adapt others for its own use. If you live in a country with a single culture, identify with only one political system/ideology, and believe that your culture no longer absorbs other cultures, this is one perspective. But it doesn't represent the whole world, not all the time.

comments.author: @Steve-pq7cb
comments.text: It may be accurate to say Han people as the emperor of China had been interrupted 3 times but every non Han Chinese emperor actually continued to use the Chinese language, Chinese traditions and Chinese culture to rule the expanded country. So China as a Chinese civilization has never been interrupted.  In other words the conquerors had always been assimilated and became Chinese. Ironically, China got bigger every time during this interruption.  It says it all how great the Chinese civilization is.

replies.author: @sea-h2j
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K Just as Britain has long had royal families from Northern Europe, France, and Germany, and Russia's Tsars were Vikings and Germans, their cultures have not actually been broken.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The assimilation argument is the strongest case for continuity — every conqueror eventually got absorbed rather than replaced. The 'reset' framing points at political ruptures, not cultural ones. Which do you think is the more meaningful kind of break for a civilization: losing its rulers, or losing its language?

The West should consider transitioning away from plutocracy, where oligarchs dictate policy, toward a meritocracy—a governing model proven to prioritize the common good and deliver consistent results.

comments.author: @weepingcamel1
comments.text: people underestimate the significance of the "mandate of heaven", before 1000 bc from kings to peasants all had to "please heaven" ie. religious power superseded political power. since 1000bc, the central government had "mandate of heaven" ie. central government superseded religious power as long as the emperor's rule is "just". This is how religion never grew as a parallel center of power, unlike in Europe.

comments.author: @userwsyz
comments.text: China has reset itself more than three times. The central kingdom started from the Zhou dynasty. At the late stage of Zhou, the nobles became warlords until emperor Qin reset China by centralized government. And then the Han dynasty continued the centralized government system, and was the most powerful empire in the time period. Several hundred years later, China reset itself by reunifying China and became the most powerful empire again in the Tang dynasty. Then the Yuan Dynasty, the Ming dynasty and the Qing dynasty, all reset China by unifying the land under one centralized government. After the “a hundred year of humiliation”, China reset itself once more to become the number one economy in the world again. So Zhou, Han, Tang, Yuan, Ming, Qing and now the people’s republic of China, that’s at least six resets. But the Chinese culture set in the Zhou dynasty did not change, and the Chinese language set in the Shang dynasty did not change.

replies.author: @知-k3q
replies.text: 90% of the grassland history is the Han nationality's colonial history.😏From Shang Dynasty to Zhou Dynasty, the system of human sacrifice was maintained in 2000 years, and the grassland was the human farm and slaughterhouse by Han people.After the great civil war at the end of the Warring States period and the abolition of the human sacrifice system, the population of grassland rose rapidly to form a unified Xiongnu.Then it was wiped out by the Han Empire and entered the 1000-year colonial era from Han Dynasty to Tang Dynasty.Only when the Han people had an extreme civil war did the weak nomadic tribes have a chance to rise, but they were wiped out and colonized at will by the unified Han people.Xianbei people were recorded at the end of the Warring States period, but they were colonized by the Han people for 600 years before they rose up in the Great Civil War of the Han people. Turks were also unified grasslands during the Civil War of the Han people, and as a result, three Turkic khanates were destroyed by the Tang Empire in 10 years.Then there are the Mongols. The earliest record was formed in the middle of the Tang Dynasty, and it was not until the Jin Dynasty that they became independent from the Han people.

replies.author: @Thanatar13
replies.text:
@kumababara1631 Rome has become a cornerstone, but it has not remained as a coherent nation. Everyone in the west (and even the Ottomans) was eager to claim the prestige of Rome- that is, its' glory- but it did not remain as a coherent nation-state. Instead, it was claimed by Germanic conquerors who then slaughtered their own kin and neighbors to spread their influence (northern crusades, Charlemagne's genocides of Saxons, etc) and then their descendants went on to create more brutal and genocidal conquests elsewhere (European and American colonialism). But the nation of Rome died in the west when Rome fell, in the east when the east Romans (who were more Hellenistic than Latin-Roman) fell, and Italy would remain fragmented for a millennia.
It makes sense, when you look at the history of Rome proper- they refused to give even their fellow Italians Roman citizenship until forced otherwise, a third of the empire were slaves (and estimated 60% of those in the city of Rome proper at its height), from their salting of Carthage to their killing a third of Gaul and enslaving another third, to what started the chain of events that finally did them in (forcing Germanic auxillary tribes into swamps where they had to trade their own children for dog meat)... such cruelty can make for fine conquerors (like the Mongols) but it will not win over hearts and minds (and so Rome fractured, just like the Mongols)

replies.author: @Elephant_King_Gj
replies.text:
@dthomas99  I think that confuses political unity with civilizational continuity.
Yes, India was often divided into kingdoms, regions, languages, and local cultures…just like China. But a civilization does not require one continuous centralized state. Ancient Greece was politically divided too, but no one says Greek civilization did not exist.
India’s continuity was carried through Sanskrit, the Vedas, epics, pilgrimage, sacred geography, temples, ritual practice, philosophical schools, yoga, Vedanta, bhakti, and shared civilizational memory.
The British did not invent Indian civilization. They consolidated a modern colonial state over an already ancient civilizational world.

replies.author: @igoldenage
replies.text: India had never been a country until the British came and brought the hundreds of countries on the  Indian Subcontinent into one.

replies.author: @dthomas99
replies.text: India is more a collection of kingdoms or chiefdoms (as exists till today), no universal language or culture, thousands of gods and deities. So it is not one continuous civilization. The 200 years of Brit colonisation and separation of Pak and Bangladesh further fracture that continuity.

replies.author: @Elephant_King_Gj
replies.text:
@WhereonEarth4K  Yes, that is a sophisticated question, because there are differences not only in outcomes, but also in mechanisms — and perhaps even in civilizational direction.  China and India are both fascinating realms, worthy of respect, study, and appreciation.
Thank you for your content.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That comparison deserved more space than this film gave it — India's continuity through the Vedic tradition into modern practice is its own extraordinary story. The question worth debating: do China and India endure in the same way, or through fundamentally different mechanisms?

replies.author: @Elephant_King_Gj
replies.text:
@MinSooi2Choo  That comment is racialized and historically careless. Civilizational continuity is not measured by whether people have some imagined pure facial type. By that standard, almost no ancient civilization on earth “survived.”

India, like China, has had migrations, mixture, change, invasion, reform, decline, revival, and continuity. That is what civilizations do over thousands of years.

Modern Indians are not simply “Persian and Central Asian immigrants.” Genetic studies show deep South Asian continuity mixed with other ancient streams, including Iranian-related and later Steppe/Central Asian ancestry. Mixture is not disappearance.

Vedic Sanskrit, the Vedas, temple traditions, yoga, Vedanta, bhakti, pilgrimage, ritual, music, and philosophy did not vanish. They continued, changed, and survived into modern India.

Reducing Indian/Vedic civilization to caste distortion while ignoring the Vedas and the entire long history of Indian spiritual and philosophical culture is not serious history.

My point is not to compare India against China. I love China deeply. My point is that ancient India also has one of the deepest continuing civilizational streams on earth.

replies.author: @MinSooi2Choo
replies.text:
Look at the physical features and facial features of current "Indians". Look at their DNA. They are basically immigrants from Persia and Central Asia. 

The population of ancient India is gone. The writings and languages is gone. Hinduism is twisted, used, misused, abused to become a caste system.
